CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (neuralizing the entity grid and proposing Grid-CNN variants). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples are generally informative. A few presentation choices reduce clarity: the exclusion of the Distributed Sentence model from final tables and the limited explanation for selecting only three Extended Grid features could be better justified, and code availability is not mentioned. From a reproducibility-oriented reader's perspective, these are minor but noteworthy clarity gaps. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (neuralizing the entity grid via CNNs and the Extended Grid-CNN) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables (results on WSJ and DUC03, task metrics) effectively convey empirical claims, though excluding the Li & Hovy baseline from the final table and limiting Extended Grid features to three are presentation choices that could use clearer justification. Overall the writing is adequate with few grammatical issues, but a bit more explicit explanation of why some experiments were omitted and whether code is available would improve reproducibility and clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (neuralizing the entity grid and proposing Grid-CNN variants). Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples are generally informative. A few presentation choices reduce clarity: the exclusion of the Distributed Sentence model from final tables and the limited explanation for selecting only three Extended Grid features could be better justified, and code availability is not mentioned. From a reproducibility-oriented reader's perspective, these are minor but noteworthy clarity gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (neuralizing the entity grid via CNNs and the Extended Grid-CNN) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables (results on WSJ and DUC03, task metrics) effectively convey empirical claims, though excluding the Li & Hovy baseline from the final table and limiting Extended Grid features to three are presentation choices that could use clearer justification. Overall the writing is adequate with few grammatical issues, but a bit more explicit explanation of why some experiments were omitted and whether code is available would improve reproducibility and clarity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an established NLP problem (local discourse coherence) using neural methods (Grid-CNN/Extended Grid-CNN) and evaluates on standard datasets (WSJ, DUC) with accepted tasks and metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating), so it squarely targets the NLP/ML community. Experimental setup and baselines are appropriate, but there are minor scope concerns: one competitor (Li & Hovy) was excluded from final tables due to poor performance, code availability is not mentioned, and the extended model only uses a subset of features—issues that affect reproducibility and completeness but not venue relevance. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within the NLP/ML venue: it proposes a neuralization of the well-known entity-grid discourse representation using CNNs, trains end-to-end with ranking loss, and evaluates on standard coherence benchmarks (WSJ, DUC) against established baselines. The methodology and evaluation follow community standards and the topic will interest the conference audience working on discourse/coherence and representation learning. My only reservation is that the contribution is an architectural adaptation of an existing representation rather than a broad new theoretical paradigm for discourse, so while highly relevant it is a slightly incremental fit for the very top tier.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an established NLP problem (local discourse coherence) using neural methods (Grid-CNN/Extended Grid-CNN) and evaluates on standard datasets (WSJ, DUC) with accepted tasks and metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating), so it squarely targets the NLP/ML community. Experimental setup and baselines are appropriate, but there are minor scope concerns: one competitor (Li & Hovy) was excluded from final tables due to poor performance, code availability is not mentioned, and the extended model only uses a subset of features—issues that affect reproducibility and completeness but not venue relevance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the NLP/ML venue: it proposes a neuralization of the well-known entity-grid discourse representation using CNNs, trains end-to-end with ranking loss, and evaluates on standard coherence benchmarks (WSJ, DUC) against established baselines. The methodology and evaluation follow community standards and the topic will interest the conference audience working on discourse/coherence and representation learning. My only reservation is that the contribution is an architectural adaptation of an existing representation rather than a broad new theoretical paradigm for discourse, so while highly relevant it is a slightly incremental fit for the very top tier.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated novelty: neuralizing the classic entity-grid representation with convolutional networks and learned entity embeddings, which is a non-trivial methodological extension beyond prior sparse-grid and graph approaches (Elsner & Charniak; Guinaudeau & Strube). The problem (local coherence) is well-studied, but the end-to-end CNN treatment that captures longer-range entity transitions and integrates entity-specific distributed features is a clear technical contribution that would inform experts. The work is not a radical new problem formulation, however, so its originality is substantial but not groundbreaking. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and clearly differentiated contribution by neuralizing the classic entity-grid with a convolutional architecture trained end-to-end (pairwise ranking) and by proposing an Extended Grid-CNN that injects entity-specific distributed features—moves that go beyond prior symbolic/grid and graph-based approaches. While local coherence assessment is a well-studied problem, the use of CNNs over distributed grid representations and the integration of entity-role embeddings is a non-trivial methodological extension that would be informative to experts. The approach and reported state-of-the-art gains distinguish it sufficiently from prior entity-grid and graph models without being a radical departure from existing paradigms.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated novelty: neuralizing the classic entity-grid representation with convolutional networks and learned entity embeddings, which is a non-trivial methodological extension beyond prior sparse-grid and graph approaches (Elsner & Charniak; Guinaudeau & Strube). The problem (local coherence) is well-studied, but the end-to-end CNN treatment that captures longer-range entity transitions and integrates entity-specific distributed features is a clear technical contribution that would inform experts. The work is not a radical new problem formulation, however, so its originality is substantial but not groundbreaking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and clearly differentiated contribution by neuralizing the classic entity-grid with a convolutional architecture trained end-to-end (pairwise ranking) and by proposing an Extended Grid-CNN that injects entity-specific distributed features—moves that go beyond prior symbolic/grid and graph-based approaches. While local coherence assessment is a well-studied problem, the use of CNNs over distributed grid representations and the integration of entity-role embeddings is a non-trivial methodological extension that would be informative to experts. The approach and reported state-of-the-art gains distinguish it sufficiently from prior entity-grid and graph models without being a radical departure from existing paradigms.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experimental results showing Grid-CNN variants outperform prior baselines on discrimination and summary-rating tasks. However, experimental methodology has some weaknesses: a promising baseline (Li & Hovy) was excluded from final tables due to poor performance on the authors’ setup, there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance, and code availability is unspecified, which hinders reproducibility. Mathematical content is minimal and appears correct for the model descriptions. Overall the conclusions are reasonable but would be strengthened by fuller ablations (e.g., more Extended Grid features), multiple seeds/statistical tests, and ensuring fair baseline implementations. | B(4): The paper's main claims are generally supported by experimental evidence showing SOTA results on discrimination and summary-rating tasks using WSJ and DUC data, and the CNN formulation is plausible and implementable (no formal proofs needed). However, there are methodological concerns: one competitive baseline (Li & Hovy) was excluded from final tables due to poor performance without detailed analysis, and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or full controls for insertion performance differences. The claim that the model captures long-range entity transitions via convolutional feature learning is reasonable but under-specified (CNNs are primarily local), and the Extended Grid experiments use only a limited subset of entity-specific features, which weakens generality of the conclusions. Overall the technical work is solid with minor issues in experimental completeness and in the strength of some interpretive claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experimental results showing Grid-CNN variants outperform prior baselines on discrimination and summary-rating tasks. However, experimental methodology has some weaknesses: a promising baseline (Li & Hovy) was excluded from final tables due to poor performance on the authors’ setup, there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance, and code availability is unspecified, which hinders reproducibility. Mathematical content is minimal and appears correct for the model descriptions. Overall the conclusions are reasonable but would be strengthened by fuller ablations (e.g., more Extended Grid features), multiple seeds/statistical tests, and ensuring fair baseline implementations.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are generally supported by experimental evidence showing SOTA results on discrimination and summary-rating tasks using WSJ and DUC data, and the CNN formulation is plausible and implementable (no formal proofs needed). However, there are methodological concerns: one competitive baseline (Li & Hovy) was excluded from final tables due to poor performance without detailed analysis, and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or full controls for insertion performance differences. The claim that the model captures long-range entity transitions via convolutional feature learning is reasonable but under-specified (CNNs are primarily local), and the Extended Grid experiments use only a limited subset of entity-specific features, which weakens generality of the conclusions. Overall the technical work is solid with minor issues in experimental completeness and in the strength of some interpretive claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experiments are adequate to demonstrate the model improves discrimination and summary coherence on WSJ and DUC'03 and include comparisons to several prior baselines, with hyperparameters reported. However, important analyses are missing: ablations/sensitivity studies are limited (the Extended Grid-CNN only tests a subset of features and there is no systematic isolation of architecture vs. input representation), insertion gains are weak and the model was not tuned for that task, and negative results (e.g., exclusion of Li & Hovy) limit transparency. Reproducibility is hampered by no mention of code release, random seeds, or variance across runs, so while the core claims are supported, the experimental rigor falls short of being fully comprehensive. | B(4): The experiments cover multiple standard coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary rating) on WSJ and DUC'03 and compare to strong baselines, giving solid empirical support for the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited: the Extended Grid-CNN only incorporates three Extended Grid features and the paper notes it wasn’t trained for insertion, so some design choices and task-specific behavior remain underexplored. Related work appears thoroughly discussed and several limitations/negative results are acknowledged (smaller insertion gains, excluded Li & Hovy results), but code availability is not mentioned which weakens reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Overall this is strong empirical work with minor gaps in ablation and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate to demonstrate the model improves discrimination and summary coherence on WSJ and DUC'03 and include comparisons to several prior baselines, with hyperparameters reported. However, important analyses are missing: ablations/sensitivity studies are limited (the Extended Grid-CNN only tests a subset of features and there is no systematic isolation of architecture vs. input representation), insertion gains are weak and the model was not tuned for that task, and negative results (e.g., exclusion of Li & Hovy) limit transparency. Reproducibility is hampered by no mention of code release, random seeds, or variance across runs, so while the core claims are supported, the experimental rigor falls short of being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments cover multiple standard coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary rating) on WSJ and DUC'03 and compare to strong baselines, giving solid empirical support for the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited: the Extended Grid-CNN only incorporates three Extended Grid features and the paper notes it wasn’t trained for insertion, so some design choices and task-specific behavior remain underexplored. Related work appears thoroughly discussed and several limitations/negative results are acknowledged (smaller insertion gains, excluded Li & Hovy results), but code availability is not mentioned which weakens reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Overall this is strong empirical work with minor gaps in ablation and reproducibility.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the most relevant prior coherence models (Elsner & Charniak’s grids, Guinaudeau & Strube’s graph model) on standard datasets (WSJ, DUC’03) and uses appropriate task metrics, which indicates generally fair and relevant comparisons. However, the authors exclude the Li & Hovy distributed sentence model from final tables due to poor performance on their setup and only incorporate a restricted subset of Extended Grid features, both of which limit the completeness of the comparison; additionally, code availability and some reproducibility details are not mentioned. Given these minor but important omissions, the empirical comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against core and relevant baselines from the entity-grid and graph-based coherence literature (Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube) on the same WSJ/DUC datasets and standard metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating), and reports hyperparameters to support fair comparisons. It also acknowledges prior methods when performance is comparable and discusses limits (smaller gains on insertion, limited subset of extended-grid features). Minor weaknesses are the exclusion of the Li & Hovy distributed sentence model from the final results table despite poor performance and the simplified Extended Grid setup, which slightly limit completeness but do not undermine overall fairness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the most relevant prior coherence models (Elsner & Charniak’s grids, Guinaudeau & Strube’s graph model) on standard datasets (WSJ, DUC’03) and uses appropriate task metrics, which indicates generally fair and relevant comparisons. However, the authors exclude the Li & Hovy distributed sentence model from final tables due to poor performance on their setup and only incorporate a restricted subset of Extended Grid features, both of which limit the completeness of the comparison; additionally, code availability and some reproducibility details are not mentioned. Given these minor but important omissions, the empirical comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against core and relevant baselines from the entity-grid and graph-based coherence literature (Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube) on the same WSJ/DUC datasets and standard metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating), and reports hyperparameters to support fair comparisons. It also acknowledges prior methods when performance is comparable and discusses limits (smaller gains on insertion, limited subset of extended-grid features). Minor weaknesses are the exclusion of the Li & Hovy distributed sentence model from the final results table despite poor performance and the simplified Extended Grid setup, which slightly limit completeness but do not undermine overall fairness.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles an important problem—modeling local discourse coherence—and introduces a clear technical contribution by neuralizing the entity grid with CNNs, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard coherence tasks (WSJ, DUC03). From an experimentalist perspective the impact is moderate: the approach is a useful advance that will likely be built upon (end-to-end CNN over entity grids, extensible entity features), but the evaluation is limited to standard corpora, some tasks (insertion) show smaller gains and only a subset of extended features were explored, and code availability is not reported. Thus the work is practically valuable to the coherence subfield but not transformative across NLP more broadly. | B(4): The paper addresses a core problem—local discourse coherence—by successfully neuralizing the influential entity-grid framework and demonstrating state-of-the-art gains on standard tasks (WSJ discrimination, insertion, DUC summary ratings). Framing entity transitions as learnable convolutional patterns and incorporating entity-specific embeddings is a conceptually useful shift that is likely to influence how researchers model coherence (moving from hand-crafted grids to end-to-end learned representations). While the work is focused on local coherence benchmarks and uses limited datasets, it plausibly opens new research directions (neural discourse models, richer entity features) and is likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—modeling local discourse coherence—and introduces a clear technical contribution by neuralizing the entity grid with CNNs, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard coherence tasks (WSJ, DUC03). From an experimentalist perspective the impact is moderate: the approach is a useful advance that will likely be built upon (end-to-end CNN over entity grids, extensible entity features), but the evaluation is limited to standard corpora, some tasks (insertion) show smaller gains and only a subset of extended features were explored, and code availability is not reported. Thus the work is practically valuable to the coherence subfield but not transformative across NLP more broadly.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a core problem—local discourse coherence—by successfully neuralizing the influential entity-grid framework and demonstrating state-of-the-art gains on standard tasks (WSJ discrimination, insertion, DUC summary ratings). Framing entity transitions as learnable convolutional patterns and incorporating entity-specific embeddings is a conceptually useful shift that is likely to influence how researchers model coherence (moving from hand-crafted grids to end-to-end learned representations). While the work is focused on local coherence benchmarks and uses limited datasets, it plausibly opens new research directions (neural discourse models, richer entity features) and is likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

main_claims: Proposes a neural local coherence model that neuralizes the entity grid representation using convolutional neural networks.
The model captures long-range entity transitions and can incorporate entity-specific features via distributed representations and convolutional feature learning.

The proposed model (Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN) achieves state-of-the-art results, outperforming existing baselines on three coherence assessment tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating).
_summary.methodology: A convolutional neural network operating over the entity grid representation of a text, trained end-to-end with a pairwise ranking objective; an extended variant incorporates entity-specific features by attaching feature values to grammatical role tokens in the grid.
datasets: Wall Street Journal (WSJ) portion of the Penn Treebank
baselines: Distributed sentence model (Li & Hovy, 2014) - experimented but excluded from final results table
metrics: Summary coherence rating (pairwise ranking against human judgments)
limitations: Gains on the insertion task are smaller than for discrimination; authors note the model was not trained specifically for insertion which may limit performance on that task.
Extended Grid-CNN experiments include only a subset (three) of the Extended Grid’s entity-specific features to keep the model simple.
The Li & Hovy distributed sentence model performed poorly (19.34% accuracy) on their WSJ setup and was excluded from result tables.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - local coherence modeling / discourse coherence

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with 10+ years building and evaluating neural NLP systems; experience releasing reproducible codebases and running large-scale ablation studies for conferences like ICLR and ACL.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental setup details (hyperparameters, seeds, data splits, code availability)
values thorough ablations that isolate contributions (e.g., architecture vs. input representation vs. training objective)
looks for statistically sound evaluation (multiple runs, variance reporting, appropriate statistical tests)
checks baseline implementations and fairness of comparisons (same data preprocessing, parameter counts, training budget)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the empirical rigor and reproducibility of the experiments, asking for targeted ablations and clarity to ensure claimed gains are well-founded.

reviewer_b.name: Discourse & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a track record on discourse, coherence modeling, and intrinsic/extrinsic evaluation of discourse-aware models across NLP tasks.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior discourse and entity-grid literature and how conceptual ideas advance the field
values clarity on linguistic assumptions, applicability to downstream tasks, and limits of the proposed representation
examines how well the model captures discourse phenomena (e.g., entity transitions, topic shifts) and whether evaluation tasks reflect real-world impact
interested in interpretability of learned features and whether insights generalize beyond benchmark datasets
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper primarily on conceptual novelty, relevance to discourse research, and potential broader impact, while probing whether empirical claims map to meaningful advances in coherence modeling.
